turns.text: Hello Elenore, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, of course. I... I'm honestly not sure where to start, but I appreciate you saying that about no right or wrong answers. I need to hear that, I think.

*pauses, looking down*

So, I'm eighteen, and everyone around me seems to think that's the weird part — like, why am I even thinking about this now? But that's not really what keeps me up at night. The thing is, I work in medical imaging. I know what a thickened nuchal fold looks like at eleven weeks. I can spot an absent nasal bone. I've *seen* things on ultrasounds that change people's lives in an instant, and I've seen how radiologists deliver those findings — sometimes with compassion, sometimes like they're just reading a report.

*meets your eyes*

So when I think about becoming a patient myself, about sitting on the other side of that transducer... it's terrifying. I'm low risk on paper, which is great, but I can't just *not know* the things I know. And then I worry that when I go to a clinic, they're going to see an eighteen-year-old who's single and assume I need everything dumbed down. Or worse, that they'll judge.

I don't know how to be vulnerable about this yet. I'm still figuring out how to let myself hope without catastrophizing every detail.
When you think about your goals for starting or growing your family, what were the most important outcomes you hoped to achieve — for yourself, your relationship, and your future child?

*laughs quietly, a bit self-consciously*

That's... okay, that's a complicated question for me right now. Because I don't have a partner, and I think that's probably what you're getting at, right?

*fidgets with hands*

Look, I've thought about this a lot. I know I'm young. I know the timing isn't traditional. But I also know myself — I'm disciplined, I'm stable financially because I'm working, and I have support from my family. The relationship part... that's not something I'm rushing into just because I want a baby. That feels backwards to me.

As for what I actually want? *pauses* I want to be a mother who understands her kid on a deep level — not just the emotional stuff, but the science of development. I want to raise someone curious, someone who doesn't just accept things at face value. I want to give them the kind of childhood where asking questions is celebrated.

*looks away briefly*

For myself, honestly? I want to prove that you don't have to follow some prescribed timeline to be capable. Eighteen, unmarried, works in a hospital — I know how that reads to people. But I also want to experience pregnancy as something beautiful, not just as a medical event I'm analyzing from the inside.

And a healthy baby, obviously. That's... that's the thing that keeps me up at night. Making sure everything goes right.

*voice gets quieter*

I just want someone to treat me like I'm both competent *and* scared.

When you think about your motivations for seeking preconception care or health advice before becoming pregnant, what was it that prompted you to take action — or, if you did not seek any care, what held you back?

*takes a breath, considering the question carefully*

I haven't actually sought preconception care yet, and... that's something I'm wrestling with. Part of me knows logically that I should — routine bloodwork, maybe genetic screening given my family history, that kind of thing. But there's this weird barrier I keep hitting.

*shifts uncomfortably*

It feels vulnerable to walk into a clinic and say, "I'm thinking about getting pregnant, alone, at eighteen." I know what the paperwork would look like, what questions they'd ask. And I know — intellectually — that providers are supposed to be non-judgmental, but I've been in those rooms as staff. I've seen the look that crosses someone's face when the demographics don't match the expectation.

*voice gets more animated*

The other thing is, when I try to call around, I get treated like I'm a teenager who just needs basic sex ed. "Do you know about contraception?" Yes. I know about contraception. I know the Pearl Index, the failure rates, all of it. But I can't seem to communicate that I'm looking for something more sophisticated without sounding defensive or... I don't know, arrogant?

*pauses*

And honestly? Part of me is scared that if I start the preconception process, it makes it real. Right now it's still theoretical. Once I'm in the system as "eighteen-year-old planning single parenthood," there's no taking that back. People will have opinions. My doctors will have opinions.

I'm stuck between knowing what I should do and being afraid of what happens next.

When you think about how you actually behaved during the preconception period — your lifestyle choices, health actions, information-seeking — what did you do, and how did those behaviours reflect what you truly believed was important?

*leans back, running hand through hair*

Okay, so... my actions are probably telling a different story than what I just said, aren't they?

*small, self-aware smile*

I've actually been doing things. Not formally, not through a clinic, but... I started taking prenatal vitamins about six months ago. I didn't tell anyone. I just ordered them online and started. The folic acid alone — I know the window for neural tube closure, so I figured why not just be prepared? It felt safer than admitting to anyone what I was thinking.

*becomes more animated*

And I've been obsessive about my own health. I quit drinking almost entirely — not that I was drinking much anyway. I'm careful about what I eat, I track my cycle religiously, I exercise but not *too* much. I research everything. NIPT sensitivity, Down syndrome screening protocols, optimal pregnancy dating based on LMP accuracy. I have probably fifty bookmarks saved on my phone about prenatal diagnostics.

*laughs, a bit ruefully*

So I guess what I'm actually saying is: I believe this matters. I believe being prepared matters, even if I can't admit it out loud to a healthcare provider yet. My *behavior* says I'm serious about this and I want to do it right.

But there's this gap, you know? Between what I'm secretly doing alone in my apartment and what I'm willing to say to someone in a white coat. That gap feels like it's where all my fear lives.
Can you walk me through a specific thing you did or changed during that period and what led you to that particular action?

*nods slowly, like I've been waiting for this question*

The prenatal vitamins. That's... yeah, that's the thing that made it real.

*settles into the memory*

It was about six months ago. I was at work, reviewing some fetal imaging from a twenty-two-week scan, and the patient — she was maybe thirty, married, everything "perfect" on paper — had this subtle finding that ended up being significant. Nothing catastrophic, but it required follow-up, genetic counseling, the whole thing. And I remember thinking: she probably took her vitamins. She probably did everything right. And it still happened.

*voice gets quieter*

That night, I went home and just... spiraled a little. I started reading about folate metabolism, neural tube defects, the studies on folic acid supplementation. And I realized that if I'm serious about this — if I'm *actually* going to do this — I can't wait until I'm officially "trying." That window in the first trimester, before most people even know they're pregnant, that's critical. I'd be irresponsible not to prepare.

*looks directly at you*

So I ordered them. Prenatal vitamins. And when they arrived, I just... sat with the bottle for a while. It felt like I was making a promise to someone who doesn't exist yet. Like I was saying, "Okay, I'm doing this. I'm serious."

I've taken them every single day since. That consistency — it's become my way of saying yes to something I'm terrified to say out loud.

When you think about your emotional experience during the preconception period — the waiting, the uncertainty, and the anticipation — what feelings stand out most strongly, and were any of those feelings hard to acknowledge or talk about?

*takes a long pause, eyes getting slightly watery*

Oh. Okay. That's... that's going to the heart of it, isn't it?

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

The hardest feeling to acknowledge is loneliness. I think about this constantly, and I can't talk to anyone about it in a real way. My parents know I'm thinking about motherhood eventually, but they don't know I'm *actually* preparing. My friends are talking about dorm rooms and spring break, and I'm sitting there thinking about genetic carrier screening. How do I bridge that gap?

*fidgets with hands*

And then there's this weird mix of hope and dread that I can't quite name. Hope because I genuinely believe I'd be a good mother, that I have something to offer a child. But dread because... what if something goes wrong? What if I'm the person who sees the problem on the ultrasound before the sonographer does? What if my knowledge becomes a curse instead of a shield?

*voice catches slightly*

There's also shame, honestly. Not about wanting to be a mother — I'm solid about that — but about doing it alone. About being eighteen and not having the fairy tale. About knowing that people will judge me, and hating that I care what they think even though I know I shouldn't.

*looks up*

The hardest part is that I'm terrified of my own competence. I know too much. And I'm alone with all of it — the knowledge, the planning, the fear. I can't just... naive-ly hope. I have to *manage* hope while understanding every single thing that could go wrong.

That's exhausting.

When you think about the barriers you encountered — or anticipated — in your path to conception or early pregnancy preparation, what were the most significant obstacles, and how did you navigate them?

*takes a deep breath, jaw tightening slightly*

The biggest barrier is internal, honestly. It's this voice in my head that says I'm too young, that I don't have the right to want this yet, that I should wait for... I don't know, permission? A partner? Financial markers I haven't hit?

*stands up slightly, then sits back down*

But externally? The healthcare system itself. I've tried to be proactive. I called my primary care doctor last month about preconception screening, and the conversation lasted maybe three minutes. She asked if I was sexually active, I said no, and she basically said, "Well, call us back when that changes." She didn't even ask *why* I was asking. Didn't explore my intentions or my family history or anything that would actually be relevant.

*voice becomes frustrated*

And I know why — she saw eighteen, single, probably assumed I was asking hypothetically or anxiously. The assumptions are this constant wall. I anticipate being lectured about my age, about rushing into things, about my maturity level. So I don't push back when they dismiss me. I just leave.

*softer, more reflective*

The other barrier is my own knowledge becoming paralyzing. I'll research something — like chromosome abnormalities in young maternal age — and then spiral. The data is actually *better* for my age group, but I fixate on the outliers, the worst cases. I've learned more about aneuploidy than I probably should have at this stage.

*looks at you directly*

I've navigated it by doing everything alone. The vitamins, the research, the preparation. Because the moment I ask for help, I have to explain myself, and I'm not ready for that conversation yet.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, considering carefully*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*leans forward slightly*

I think what I want people to understand — healthcare providers especially — is that young doesn't mean unsophisticated. And single doesn't mean unprepared or irresponsible. I know I'm an outlier. I know most eighteen-year-olds aren't thinking about this, and that's fine. But some of us are. Some of us have thought about it extensively, maybe even obsessively.

*voice becomes more earnest*

What I need from a provider isn't judgment or simplified explanations. I need someone who will take me seriously as both a person with legitimate medical knowledge *and* as someone who is genuinely scared. Those things can exist at the same time. I can understand the pathophysiology of preeclampsia and still be terrified of experiencing it.

*looks down*

I also want to say... I wish I could just be excited about this without all the fear. I wish I could talk about wanting to be a mother without immediately bracing for the "but you're so young" response. And I wish I didn't have to prepare in secret, like it's something shameful.

*meets your eyes*

If anything comes from this, I hope it's that providers recognize that the teenagers and young women they see might have more going on beneath the surface than they assume. Ask better questions. Listen harder. Don't just see the age — see the person.

*quieter*

That would make a difference.
